questions.question: What is the collective name for the trillions of microorganisms living in your gut?
questions.explanation: The microbiome is the collective name for the trillions of microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, and fungi) living in your gut that influence everything from vitamin production to neurotransmitter creation.

questions.question: What is dysbiosis?

options.text: A healthy balance of gut bacteria
A state where unhelpful bacteria have taken over, producing inflammatory substances
A digestive enzyme found in the stomach

questions.explanation: Dysbiosis is a state of microbial imbalance where unhelpful bacteria have taken over the gut. In this state, they can produce inflammatory substances that send stress signals to your brain.

questions.question: What is the difference between prebiotics and probiotics?

options.text: Prebiotics are live bacteria; probiotics are types of fiber
They are the same thing with different names
Prebiotics are fiber that feeds good bacteria; probiotics are live beneficial bacteria in fermented foods
Prebiotics are found in meat; probiotics are found in dairy only

questions.explanation: Prebiotics are types of dietary fiber (the fertilizer) that feed your beneficial gut bacteria, while probiotics are live, beneficial bacteria (the new seeds) found in fermented foods like yogurt, kefir, and sauerkraut.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a prebiotic-rich food?
questions.explanation: Garlic is a prebiotic-rich food, meaning it contains types of dietary fiber that your beneficial gut bacteria love to eat. Other prebiotic foods include onions, bananas, oats, and asparagus. Yogurt, kombucha, and sauerkraut are probiotic (fermented) foods.

questions.question: What simple dietary goal was suggested for improving gut bacteria diversity?

options.text: Eliminate all sugar from your diet
Eat 30 different plant foods per week

questions.explanation: The lesson highlighted a simple but powerful goal: eat 30 different plant foods per week. This includes herbs, spices, nuts, seeds, fruits, and vegetables. Diversifying your plant intake increases the diversity of your beneficial gut bacteria.
